title: systemctl stop X does not remove container from systemd unit, but podman stop X removes it from the command line
body:
### Issue Description

I have a systemd .service file containing
```
[Service]
OOMScoreAdjust=-900
Nice=-5

Type=forking
TimeoutStartSec=280
TimeoutStopSec=320
ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/podman run -d --cap-add=IPC_LOCK --log-driver=none --net=host --read-only --read-only-tmpfs=false --mount type=bind,src=/etc/k,dst=/conf --tty --rm=true -a=stderr -a=stdout --userns=auto:size=10 --name=k localhost/k:2023-01-25
ExecStop=/usr/local/bin/podman stop k
StandardOutput=null
```
I do:
```
# systemctl start k
... wait, wait, wait
# systemctl stop k
... wait
# podman ps --all
```
With podman 4.4.2-dev (Git Commit: 396c138ce54c425aded8a88d58d7c7c8aa8c6560) the output now is:
```
# podman ps  --all                    
CONTAINER ID  IMAGE                   COMMAND     CREATED         STATUS                    PORTS       NAMES
09be502ebd67  localhost/k:2023-01-25              13 seconds ago  Exited (0) 2 seconds ago              k
```
The image is present, despite started with `run --rm=true`.  I remove the image (podman rm k), so that podman ps --all shows nothing.

If I call instead from the beginnig `podman stop k` inplace of `systemctl stop k`, then
```console
# podman ps  --all                    
CONTAINER ID  IMAGE       COMMAND     CREATED     STATUS      PORTS       NAMES
```
shows no leftovers.

* Calling `systemd stop k` for a service with `ExecStop=/usr/local/bin/podman stop k` terminates the container, but does not remove it, despite being started with `run --rm=true`.  Calling instead `podman stop k` from the command line does stop and remove the container.

I have no systemd podman.service running.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

see above

### Describe the results you received

see above

### Describe the results you expected

see above

comments.body:
We only support unites generated by podman generate systemd or quadlet, there are a lot of things that can go wrong with a unit like yours.
systemd will kill everything in the cgroup including the podman cleanup process which would remove the container once podman stop exited.
I suggest you just use a unit created with `podman generated systemd --new`

Thanks for your answer.  Just adding `--cidfile=%t/%n.ctr-id` to `ExecStart=podman run --cidfile=%t/%n.ctr-id …` and `ExecStop=podman stop --cidfile=%t/%n.ctr-id …` did solve the problem: on `systemctl stop k` the container is removed.

Adding
```
ExecStopPost=/usr/local/bin/podman rm \
        -f \
        --ignore -t 10 \
        --cidfile=%t/%n.ctr-id
```
, as suggested by `podman generate systemd --new k`, was not necessary,

> systemd will kill everything in the cgroup including the podman cleanup process which would remove the container once podman stop exited.

The problem is that podman stop has not removed the container, before exiting. I do not see how cgroups interfere here.

> The problem is that podman stop has not removed the container, before exiting.

but does remove the container, when --cidfile is passed to `run` and `stop`, or `podman stop` is called outside systemd-service, on the command shell.

Podman 4.4 ships with Quadlet which makes running containers in systemd significantly easier.  Feel free to have a look at the man pages (https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-systemd.unit.5.html) and upcoming blog posts on www.redhat.com/sysadmin.
